item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably: of the three domains that might have owned it, two have expired and one is notoriously greedy (cough cough Chegg). As such, the image is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Directly after the first parenthetical in the description there appears to be a typo. It says “SCP-1809 is a former arborist” when I assume it should say “SCP-1809-A is a former arborist”. Fun article overall, def +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why haven't I seen this before??!! I accidentally clicked on the "microscopic" tag on my 2978.

+1 coz is 2adorabl4me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit clunky in places, and required a ton of disbelief suspension, but the idea is so delightfully out there I don't care a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While i won;t deny that there's some definite weirdness going on, i find this amusing enough to not care. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but as Vivax points out, scientifically and tonally dodgy. -1 for throwing me out of immersion within two paragraphs. I want to upvote it, but it needs some work there. (I volunteer!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1809 refers to a collective of sapient microscopic organisms, primarily inhabiting cytoplasms within most permanent and quiescent cell types contained within one (1) human male

You mean "SCP-1809 is a collective of sapient intracellular microorganisms that have been discovered colonizing 1 male, human host. The microorganisms are capable of inhabiting the majority of cell types within their host."

will attempt to penetrate the cell membrane, invariably allowing for a safe opening to within, in such a manner so as to prevent cytoplasmic leakage.

Instead of "cytoplasmic leakage" you mean "lysis".

Once inside, SCP-1809-1 will attempt to rearrange all internal subcellular organelles such as nuclei, without physically harming the cell interior.

Which is basically not a thing you should be doing. You're going to fuck up cell polarity, tissue organization and the cytoskeleton. You are going to fuck up cell-cell junctions.

Um…instead of that they should try to like…repair tissue damage or kill tumors or something. Seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was funny enough to make me laugh but realistic enough to not be a -J. Gotta say i love this. i always like a smidge of comedy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally prefer the idea of an SCP-1809-1 taskforce dedicated to maintaining the sanctity of the neural tissue haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going for a repossession theme, which was the intended viewpoint, but I suppose it would be better left open to interpretation :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just out of curiosity, was the one individual forcibly vacated from the neuron due to the obvious issues arising out of building homes with cells that pretty much don't replace damaged ones?

The thought just occurred to me, and I was wondering if that was the intended viewpoint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… Mind if I yoink 'contagious sickle cells' as the basis idea for a future SCP? Macro scale prions. Hm.

But yeah, I definitely agree that the article would be better if the issues were addressed, if only to dismiss them with author 'BUT THEN MAGIC ANOMALOUS HAPPENED' handwaving, as long as it's done well.

Even if it were something along the lines of "Common SCP-1809 population in a given host rarely exceed # individuals, making damage caused to cells well within a healthy human's healing capabilities."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that it's entirely possible to write the article such that the effect of 1809 on the cells/body is a non-issue — either by describing the situation such that it would logically not make him sick, or simply using an author fiat to state that it does not. More generally, given that we deal with anomalies here, there are actually very few scientific issues that can't be circumvented. My concern here is that it doesn't address the problem, thus leaving a plot hole.

I suppose individual RBCs could be given the sickle-cell phenotype by means of actively converting their haemoglobin into haemoglobin S, but this would not be noticeable unless it affected much of SCP-1809-A's blood cells, including the ones that are pretty regularly made anew — and even then, that fine print specifically refers to outbreaks, and Sickle Cell disease cannot be transferred between cells. Even if many RBCs in an area got SCD from direct protein manipulation, this would not affect any other cell's chance of getting it.

I wouldn't mind a sneaky socioeconomic reference in there either, but it's hardly sneaky, and kind of jarring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's clever but it doesn't explore the concept very much to be of interest..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am so into this one. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That. I liked the cute little touches, like the red text on the red blood cell ad. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that they're contained in a single dude and they care about his well-being. (Also yes, this should be Euclid if only because he could potentially escape and spread them. Maybe.) I like that they're into real estate.

What I don't like is that picture. Maybe it's just me, but it looks like you photoshopped a manatee into it. It doesn't really add anything, I feel, and my advice is to drop it. Of course, no one else has said anything, so again, maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little silly but enjoyable with good use of science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

On principle, I upvote anything i feel would make even the most jaded Foundation researcher say, "What the shit????!!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha! Osmosis Jones… good memories. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Forgot to mention this during review
– hide block

Ozzy and Drix

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see. I went back and double checked the definition of "Euclid." One thing it says is a Euclid SCP can be unpredictable. You're right, the classification makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Depending on the areas of concentration and number of parasites in the body, and how long they live in a cell 'house' for, it could very well be a non-issue. Sickle-cell could be possibly induced in blood cells by the parasites, especially if they're messing around with the cytoskeleton or refolding ("Carving"?) proteins.

I also missed the part about cell walls. I must do penance by re-reading my biology textbooks in entirety.

I'd almost advocate trying to sneakily include a social-economic status in SCP-1809. I can imagine neurons being more highly desired 'houses' due to their supposed 'view' (And limited nature).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I classified it as Euclid purely due to the fact that the behaviour of SCP-XXXX-1 is unpredictable, especially due to the second addendum. As far as I know, Euclid classification doesn't necessarily indicate dangerousness. You never know though, I might be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind-of like this one. But my main problem with this one is its classification. Why is this classified as Euclid? I've read it three times now and I still can't find what makes this SCP dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Animal cells do not possess cell walls. You're thinking of a cell membrane.

This also fails to address how the presence of 1809 affects the cell — the very act of them entering and then forcibly altering the location of cells would most likely involve wrecking the cytoskeleton, which would cause big problems with transport, cell division, and cell rigidity. There's also the fact that those organelles are most likely where they are for a reason. Suffice to say that the guy's health would be impacted negatively, which actually wouldn't be a bad way to go with this.

Do housing ads ever actually say stuff like "suitable for the working class"? It doesn't sound like something they'd do, at least given how closely these things are mimicking humans. I think they would emphasize the low price instead, given how people don't like to be reminded of their social class.

Sickle-cell anemia is a genetic disorder that doesn't get "outbreaks" — if the host doesn't have sickle-cell, he's never going to get it. I'm not sure whether that was intentional.

I sort of like the idea, but I'm downvoting for these problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that was a minor typo. Thanks for the fix :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just noticed how much SCPs communicate with Morse Code. I wish there was some way we could work around that.

I enjoyed the concept here, so +1. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a tad absurd, but it's grounded enough in reality to be believable. An interesting concept, to be sure.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As somebody who loves biology, I thoroughly enjoyed this.

I also noticed that you were using the word 'Neurone' which is the French spelling of 'Neuron' and I went ahead and fixed that for you. If it was deliberate on your end, feel free to revert it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cellular real estate?

…Do we have any basal ganglia still available? I like to feel connected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is simply too silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god this is so stupid why does it make me laugh so much +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to LongWayHome and azzleflux for feedback. This is my first SCP postage in a while since two previous failures, so here's hoping my writing has improved somewhat since then. Thanks for any further feedback given!
